1: ʾôdĕkā bĕkol-libbî neged ʾĕlōhîm ʾăzammĕreka — "I will praise you, O LORD, with all my heart; in the presence of the heavenly assembly I will sing your praises" — bĕkol-libbî (with all my heart) — the lēb (heart) is in Hebrew thought the seat of will, intellect, and inner orientation; "all my heart" signals wholeness of devotion. neged ʾĕlōhîm (before/in the presence of gods) — the phrase is deliberately ambiguous: ʾĕlōhîm could refer to (a) pagan gods, before whom the psalmist defiantly praises YHWH; (b) the heavenly assembly or divine council (Ps 82:1; 89:7); or (c) a plural of majesty for God himself. The most probable reading in this context is (b) — the celestial assembly — making the praise a cosmic act witnessed by the heavenly court.
2: ʾeštaḥăweh ʾel-hêkal qodšĕkā wĕʾôdeh ʾet-šimĕkā ʿal ḥasdĕkā wĕʿal ʾamittĕkā kî-higdaltā ʿal-kol-šimĕkā ʾimrātĕkā — "I will bow toward your holy temple and praise your name for your steadfast love and faithfulness; for you have exalted your word above even your own name" — ḥasdĕkā wĕʾamittĕkā (your steadfast love and your faithfulness/truth) — the paired terms of covenant character throughout the Psalter (Ps 25:10; 26:3; 36:5; 57:3; 115:1). kî-higdaltā ʿal-kol-šimĕkā ʾimrātĕkā — the most difficult clause in the psalm: "you have magnified your word/promise above all your name." The most defensible reading: the fulfillment of God's specific promise (ʾimrāh, word/promise/utterance) has proven greater than even the general reputation of his name — the concrete act of salvation exceeded the expectations created by his fame. The verse does not subordinate the name to the word in ontological rank but testifies that experience of God's word-keeping surpassed what his reputation alone had led the psalmist to expect.
3: bĕyôm qārāʾtî watĕʿānēnî tarhĕbēnî bĕnapšî ʿōz — "on the day I called out, you answered me; you filled my soul with bold strength" — bĕyôm qārāʾtî watĕʿānēnî (in the day I called and you answered me) — the swift, personal response to prayer; not a delayed or conditional answer but an answered call. tarhĕbēnî bĕnapšî ʿōz — "you made my soul bold with strength" — rāhab (to be wide, broad, bold) in the Hiphil (cause to be broad): YHWH expanded the psalmist's inner capacity, creating boldness. The boldness (ʿōz, strength) is located in the nepeš — not physical strength added externally but inner fortitude produced in the whole self.
4: yôdûkā YHWH kol-malkê ʾāreṣ kî šāmĕʿû ʾimrê pîkā — "all the kings of the earth will praise you, O LORD, when they hear the words you have spoken" — kol-malkê ʾāreṣ (all the kings of the earth) — the universal horizon; what began as personal praise (vv1–3) expands to encompass world rulers. šāmĕʿû ʾimrê pîkā (when they hear the words of your mouth) — the mechanism of universal praise is hearing the divine word, not coercion. The psalmist envisions a future in which YHWH's acts are so publicly demonstrated that even Gentile kings acknowledge him (cf. Ps 22:27–28; 86:9; Isa 45:22–23).
5: wĕyāšîrû bĕdarkê YHWH kî gādôl kĕbôd YHWH — "they will sing of the paths of the LORD, for the glory of the LORD is great" — darkê YHWH (paths/ways of the LORD) — YHWH's characteristic ways of acting in the world; the pattern of his moral and redemptive governance. kĕbôd YHWH (the glory of the LORD) — kābôd (weight, heaviness, glory) — the manifest presence and honor of YHWH. The kings sing of his observable dĕrākim, which produces their recognition of his kābôd.
6: kî rām YHWH wĕšāpāl yirʾeh wĕgābōah mimmĕrāḥāq yĕdāʿ — "though the LORD is exalted, he pays attention to the humble; but the arrogant he perceives from a distance" — rām YHWH (the LORD is high/exalted) — divine transcendence. wĕšāpāl yirʾeh (but the humble/lowly he sees) — the divine reversal motif central to the Psalter and the prophets: the exalted LORD sees downward toward the šāpāl. gābōah mimmĕrāḥāq yĕdāʿ (the haughty/high from afar he knows) — the proud receive only distant surveillance, not intimate attention. The reversal inverts human expectation: height should attract the high God's notice, but lowliness does instead (Isa 57:15; 66:2).
7: ʾim-ʾēlēk bĕqereb ṣārāh tĕḥayyênî ʿal ʾap ʾōyĕbay tišlaḥ yādĕkā wĕtôšîʿēnî yĕmîněkā — "even when I walk through terrible trouble, you keep me alive; you reach out your hand against the fury of my enemies, and your right hand rescues me" — bĕqereb ṣārāh (in the midst of trouble/distress) — ṣārāh (distress, from ṣārar, to press/bind) — to walk "in the midst of" it means to be surrounded. tĕḥayyênî (you keep me alive, you revive me) — Piel of ḥāyāh, to preserve life, to quicken. ʿal ʾap ʾōyĕbay (against the anger of my enemies) — YHWH's hand is extended against the enemies' wrath. yĕmîněkā (your right hand) — the right hand of a warrior holds the sword; YHWH's right hand is his saving power (Exod 15:6; Ps 118:15–16).
8: YHWH yiglōr baʿădî YHWH ḥasdĕkā lĕʿôlām maʿăśê yādêkā ʾal-tereP — "the LORD will fulfill his purpose for me; your steadfast love, O LORD, endures forever — do not abandon what your hands have made" — yiglōr (Qal imperfect of gāmar, to complete, accomplish, bring to full term) — a rare verb: YHWH will "complete" on behalf of the psalmist. The psalm ends where it began: with ḥasdĕkā lĕʿôlām (your steadfast love forever) — the covenantal ground of all confidence. maʿăśê yādêkā (the work of your hands) — the psalmist appeals to their status as YHWH's own handiwork: you made me; do not let me be undone. ʾal-tereP (do not abandon/let go) — rāpaʾ (to relax, drop, forsake) in the Hiphil imperative; the prayer that the divine hand which made will not release.
